# Thread: TODAY'S LESSON: Just how specific is decentralized biology for cells? Biochemists have found that when living cells use
**Source:** <https://threadreaderapp.com/thread/1700200761106288936.html>
**Published:** 2023-09-09T14:04:49.000Z
**Tweets:** 25
----------------------------------------------------------------------

### Tweet 1
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700200761106288936>

TODAY'S LESSON: Just how specific is decentralized biology for cells? Biochemists have found that when living cells use chiral molecules, they use one chirality exclusively. The sugars that make up DNA backbone, for example, are all right-handed. The amino acids that make up proteins are all left-handed. Kinda of interesting don't you think? Did you know that if the wrong enantiomers slip into pharmaceuticals or supplements, the effects can sometimes be toxic or even lethal?

### Tweet 2
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700201662315372663>

Centralized scientists have been mystified by this observation since Louis Pasteur. Many centralized theories can explain why type of molecule might have become homochiral, yet, none of these centralized theories have explained why whole networks of biomolecules did. Why is that?

### Tweet 3
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700206807325507753>

They argue some event or series of events early in the history of life on Earth must have “broken the mirror,” as biochemists describe chirality, throwing life into molecular asymmetry. Centralized scientists have debated why life became homochiral, and whether it needed to happen or if it was purely a fluke. Were chiral preferences impressed on early life by biased samples of molecules arriving from space, geologic events, or did they somehow evolve out of mixtures that started out as equal parts right- and left-handed?

### Tweet 4
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700210062151561684>

Could magnetic surfaces on minerals in bodies of water on the primordial Earth, have been charged by the planet’s magnetic field? Might this have served as the “chiral stimulants” that attracted some forms of molecules more than others, kicking off a process that amplified the chirality of biological molecules, from RNA precursors all the way to proteins and beyond?

### Tweet 5
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700213453049188540>

This idea ties geophysics to geochemistry, to prebiotic chemistry, & ultimately to biochemistry on Earth. This hypothesis has actually already been backed by “actual experiments” that have been done and that “they’re doing this under realistic conditions.

### Tweet 6
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700214315574260047>

When Ron Naaman, a professor of chemical physics at the Weizmann Institute of Science in Israel, discovered a critical effect of chiral molecules and magnetic fields related to topology. [weizmann.ac.il/chembiophys/na…](https://www.weizmann.ac.il/chembiophys/naaman/home)

### Tweet 7
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700215322429522029>

Electrons are different than protons. Electrons are all the same in the cosmos but there are things that differentiate them from one another. Electrons have two key properties: They carry a negative charge, and they have “spin,” a quantum property analogous to intrinsic clockwise or counterclockwise rotation. When molecules interact with other molecules or surfaces, their electrons can redistribute themselves, polarizing the molecules by creating a negative charge at their destination and a positive charge at their starting point.

### Tweet 8
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700216317154939299>

Topology of electrons is critical in biology as the picture shows because it affects semiconductive abilities. Naaman and his team discovered that chiral molecules filter electrons based on the direction of their spin. Electrons with one spin orientation will move more efficiently across a chiral molecule in one direction than the other and this effects its ability to semiconduct. Sugars and proteins have a different chirality for a reason. It turns out that electrons with the opposite spin move more freely the other way.

### Tweet 9
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700216923097538615>

Sugars and proteins have dissimilar chirality. Both are found in DNA and RNA. The implications of this should be obvious to a decentralized clinician. Chiral molecules scatter the electrons according to their direction of rotation = imagine throwing a Frisbee that glances off the wall of a hallway. If the Frisbee hits the right-hand wall, it will bounce forward only if it’s rotating clockwise; otherwise, it will bounce backward. The opposite will happen if you hit the Frisbee off the left-hand wall. Where the electron goes depends on its quantum spin and chirality.

### Tweet 10
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700217471129534538>

IMPLICATIONS FOR DECENTRALIZED MEDICINE?
Because of that scattering, electrons with a given spin end up aggregating at one pole of a chiral molecule (and the right-handed and left-handed versions of the molecule gather opposite spins at their respective poles). But that redistribution of spins affects how the chiral molecules interact with magnetic surfaces because electrons spinning in opposite directions attract one another, and those spinning in the same direction repel one another.

Consequently, when a chiral molecule approaches a magnetic surface, it will be drawn closer if the molecule and the surface have opposite spin biases. If their spins match, they will repel each other. (Because other chemical interactions are also going on, the molecule can’t simply flip to realign itself.) So a magnetic surface can act as a chiral agent, preferentially interacting with only one enantiomer of a compound. This is critical in understanding how DNA and RNA work as an electromagnetic antenna of information transfer. It also helps us understand how water and DNA interact electromagnetically.

### Tweet 11
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700218726333722940>

In 2011, Naaman & his team measured the spin of electrons as they moved through double-stranded DNA, confirming that the CISS effect is both real and strong. [science.org/doi/10.1126/sc…](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1199339)

### Tweet 12
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700219584878383584>

This mechanism maybe how general anesthesics works in humans via the CISS effect. In electrochemical experiments, it appears that the efficiency of the oxygen reduction is reduced by the presence of general anesthetics in solution. This suggests that a spin–orbit coupling to the anesthetics depolarizes the spins. .

### Tweet 13
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700220251156201855>

At its core, homochirality isnt a biologic problem,
It looks like a physics question because it’s about symmetries.

### Tweet 14
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700221423229227101>

How does this look to physicist and not to a centralized biologist? @hubermanlab

Ultraviolet light is the only part of the terrestrial spectrum capable of ejecting many electrons from the magnetic surfaces of matter floating in the primordial seas of earth. Because of this, many of those ejected electrons would have had the same quantum spin when they were freed from the matter. The ejected electrons might then have interacted preferentially with specific enantiomers, and the resulting chemical reactions might then have preferentially assembled right-handed RNA precursors.

It is not hard to imagine a situation where there was a primordial setting in a shallow lake where there were surfaces full of magnetic minerals and the water contained a mixture of chiral precursors to nucleotides.

### Tweet 15

How might this happen? I doubt it was chemical. I bet it was physical and that bet is made on the ideas I shared with Rick Rubin and Huberman in March. Most of the biologic substrates we use have crystalline structures. Water, melanin, and hemoglobin, as well as chlorophyll. Crystallization would be the easiest way for purified collections of enantiomers to self assemble 3 billion years ago.

### Tweet 16

Naaman’s research group has already shown experimental proof that they could use magnetic surfaces to crystallize enantiomers preferentially. So this is not a hard leap to make. Once a crystal seed forms from the enantiomer that is attracted to the surface, the crystal preferentially grows by incorporating more of the same enantiomer.

### Tweet 17
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700223457231880634>

Might light ability to magnetize crystals solved the homochirality problem in biology? When researchers magnetized a surface one way, they created crystals that were purely right-handed; if they magnetized it the other way, the crystals were purely left-handed. It seems that light can do this.

### Tweet 18
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1700225479423275389>

UV light has the known ability to magnetize matter. This is how oxygen and CO2 change the quaternary structure of the hemoglobin crystals in RBCs. It turns out when chiral molecules are adsorbed onto the magnetic surfaces, they created a local zip code effect of a highly ordered magnetic field over the surface. This surface can be many times as strong as Earth’s magnetic field. Why? As scale drops within the electromagnetic force magnetic field strength increases. When UVA and IRA light hit the skin and surface blood vessels they create NO which is a magnetic free radical that changes the circulatory flow. The same is true with electric fields in mitochondria = why they have 30 million volts charges in their membranes.

It turns out you don’t need a highly magnetic field on a planet for the CISS effect to occur. All you need is UV and IR-A light to make it happen quantum mechanically..........the biological homochirality problem may be reduced to ensuring that a single common RNA precursor could be made homochiral just using sunlight. How is that for simplicity?

@hubermanlab 20. How? [pubs.aip.org/aip/jcp/articl…](https://pubs.aip.org/aip/jcp/article/159/6/061102/2905827/The-central-dogma-of-biological-homochirality-How)

Did you know that we have data published that show that analogs of right-handed transfer RNA molecules — which bind amino acids and bring them to the ribosome to make proteins — link to left-handed amino acids 10 times faster than to right-handed ones? Ya' think that this might be important in self assembly under sunlight? Can you imagine the effect of ALAN on this process now? Ya's still think light is not capable of altering your biology?

### Tweet 24

If life started in the northern hemisphere and favored molecules with one handedness, then it would have shown the opposite handedness if it had arisen in the southern hemisphere. The Earth magnetic flux has a reverse polarity between north and south.

### Tweet 25

The CISS effect needs to show one last thing before it repalces modern centralized dogma......can it polymerize RNA.

The chiral-induced spin selectivity (CISS) effect has now been established experimentally and theoretically in the literature. The National Childhood Vaccine Injury Act (NCVIA) of 1986 (42 U.S.C. §§ 300aa-1 to 300aa-34) was signed into law by United States President Ronald Reagan as part of a larger health bill on November 14, 1986. NCVIA's purpose was to eliminate the potential financial liability of vaccine manufacturers due to vaccine injury claims to ensure a stable market supply of vaccines, and to provide cost-effective arbitration for vaccine injury claims.

The inclusion of medical records was highlighted by a reporter, Joy Buchanan from the Daily Press in Newport News, Virginia, who was alerted to a clause in one medical centres' brochure for patients on privacy.

The brochure stated that the centre “may disclose medical information about you to authorised federal officials so they may without limitation (i) provide protection to the President, other authorised persons or foreign heads of state or conduct special investigations, or (ii) conduct law-ful intelligence, counter-intelligence, or other national security activities authorised by law.”

Ms Buchanan reported the story in the Daily Press on 29 December (sect A: 1).

Subsequent investigations have shown that virtually all medical centres have incorporated the same or similar language in their brochures. Under the Health Insurance Portability and Accounting Act, patients must be told that their records are private and about any exceptions to that rule. The statements are often a single paragraph in documents several pages long, which are given to patients before they see a doctor.

Michael Williams, associate professor of neurology and neurosurgery at Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, said most people in the United States don't seem to know about Section 215. “I only found out about it in June 2005 at a meeting of the American Medical Association. If patients knew about this, I think they would be bothered—or I hope they would be,” he said. “If our records can be seized with no safeguards then we're all in trouble.”

Duane Cady, chairman of the board of the American Medical Association, said that the association adopted a new policy in June 2005 “calling for modifications to the Patriot Act to protect patient confidentiality and minimise legal liability for physicians.”

Congressman Dan Lungren of California defended the provision allowing the seizure of medical records. He said it might be important to know if, for example, someone connected to an international terrorist organisation sought an anthrax vaccine. He said the government couldn't afford to be limited by the need for “probable cause.” There is an “essential difference,” he said, “between a criminal investigation to prove who committed a crime after it's occurred and the need to prevent terrorist attacks.”

Jane Orient, executive director of the Association of American Physicians and Surgeons, warned, “This war against terrorism is a totally open ended affair, and the definition of terrorism is \[overly\] broad.”

To understand the importance of this draft bill, we need to look back at the Patriot Act, rushed into law just weeks after the September 11th attacks.

BASKIN: In the name of the war on terror, the Patriot Act of 2001 allowed the government to use secret searches, wiretaps and other covert surveillance with less oversight. It broadened the government’s ability to access email and monitor the Internet and it authorized the Attorney General to lock up foreign terror suspects without a hearing.

COLE: Principally what the Patriot Act did was to expand the government’s ability to conduct surveillance on U.S. citizens and foreign nationals alike without the same kinds of judicial oversight that had previously been in existence. But much of that surveillance is conducted in secret.

BASKIN: David Cole teaches law at Georgetown University. He says in the fall of 2001, he and other advocates of civil liberties were ignored in the haste to pass the Patriot Act.

BASKIN: The Patriot Act moved through Congress with unprecedented speed. There were few public hearings and very little debate and some members now have questions about the very legislation they approved overwhelmingly. Nevertheless, the Justice Department has now drafted an even tougher sequel. Insiders are calling it, “Patriot Act II.”

BASKIN: This draft legislation has been a closely guarded secret. Cole reviewed the confidential draft at Georgetown Law School and gave us his evaluation.

COLE: I think this is quite a radical proposal. It authorizes secret arrests. Never before in this country have we authorized secret arrests. Arrests have always been public. And that’s for a good reason. To take someone off the street and lock him up is the most serious thing that a state can do to its citizenry, short of executing them.

BASKIN: And Cole says the draft legislation is worrisome because it is so broadly written.

COLE: It would give the Attorney General essentially unchecked authority to deport anyone who he thought was a danger to our economic interests. It would strip citizenship from people for lawful political associations.

BASKIN: Cole says the proposed changes in the powers of government will be very controversial.

COLE: Many of these provisions I think will trouble Republicans. Will trouble conservatives. Will trouble people who are concerned about too much government.

BASKIN: But very few people outside the Justice Department have even had a chance to see the draft bill. NOW has obtained a cover letter for the draft that shows only one member of Congress has received it: Speaker of the House Dennis Hastert.

Iowa Republican Senator Chuck Grassley is a senior member of the Judiciary Committee which would oversee this type of legislation. But when we caught up with him yesterday at Union Station, we were the first to show it to him.

BASKIN: What do you think of the Justice Department’s new proposal, it’s being called Patriot Act II?

GRASSLEY: I’m going to be very cautious about that legislation. Quite frankly I’m not going to be for dramatic expansion of it even knowing the environment of terrorism I know is now a threat to Americans. I think we need to move very cautiously. And I think we’ve had about enough expansion as we should have for a while.

BASKIN: Grassley says before Congress even considers any new type of terror legislation, he wants answers from the Justice Department about the law already passed in 2001. For months, Senators with oversight over the Patriot Act say Attorney General John Ashcroft has refused to answer key questions.

LEAHY: We really do want answers to the requests that we send. We have 23 outstanding requests to various parts of the Department of Justice dating back a year, July of last year.

SPECTER: How do we communicate with you? And are you really too busy too respond? And as I’ve already said, I’ll accept that as an answer.

GRASSLEY: You know, it’s just like pulling teeth to get information out of a lot of bureaucracy. But where it comes to this issue the Justice Department has been very difficult.

BASKIN: The Justice Department declined NOW’s request for an interview, but confirmed in a statement that additional anti-terrorism measures are under active review.

Professor Cole says if lawmakers do receive new legislative proposals, they should recall that none of the provisions of the draft bill, or the 2001 law, would have prevented the 9/11 attacks.

COLE: There’s been no showing that if the Patriot Act had been on the books, September 11th wouldn’t have happened. It was rather through a failure of analysis.

BASKIN: Given that we’re in the aftermath of a terrorist attack, wouldn’t these proposals help us fight the war on terror and protect American lives?

COLE: They might. It’s always been a balance between keeping us secure and keeping us secure from the government. Our liberties are most tested in times like this. But they were put into place precisely for times like this. So if we give them up at times like this without very careful consideration, without a very strong showing, then we’ve given up what this country stands for.

MOYERS: Chuck Lewis, whom you just saw in that piece is with me now. He is the Executive Director of the nonpartisan Center for Public Integrity, the organization responsible for obtaining that document. Chuck Lewis, thank you for joining us.

LEWIS: Thank you.

MOYERS: The Patriot Act was passed six weeks after 9/11. We know now that it greatly changed the balance between liberty and security in this nation’s framework. What do you think — what’s the significance of this new document, called the Domestic Security Enhancement Act of 2003?

LEWIS: I think the significance is it just deepens and broadens, further extends the first Patriot Act. That act in 2001, they had six weeks, which was not a lot of time to throw something together. Now there’s been 18 months of all kinds of things that have happened and court decisions that have tried to roll back some of the Patriot Act.

And other concerns, law enforcement, people have, and so they’ve had time to sift and sort what they want. And it’s arguably might be a more thorough rendering of all the things law enforcement and intelligence agencies would like to have in a perfect world. It’s sort of how I look at it, and I think it’s a very tough document when it comes to secrecy and surveillance.

I understand the concerns about fear of terrorism. And it certainly…

MOYERS: We all have those…

LEWIS: We all have those and there are things in the legislation that make sense, and that are reasonable, I think for any American. But there are other things that really take some of the Patriot Act civil liberties issues that folks were concerned about and go even further. And I think it’s gonna be very controversial. Some of these sections are gonna be debated for weeks and months.

MOYERS: So many of these powers latent in this draft legislation were powers that were taken away from the intelligence community some years ago because they were abused.

LEWIS: That’s right.

MOYERS: Do you see any protection in here against potential abuse?

LEWIS: I don’t think there’s very much — there’s a lot more authority and power for government. There’s less oversight and information about what government is doing. That’s the headline and that’s the theme. And the safeguards seem to be pretty minimal to me.

MOYERS: I just go through here, you know? It would give the Attorney General the unchecked power to deport any foreigner…

LEWIS: Right.

MOYERS: Including lawful permanent resident aliens. It would give the government the power to keep certain arrests secret until an indictment is found never in our history have we permitted secret arrests. It would give the government power to bypass courts and grand juries in order to conduct surveillance without a judge’s permission. I mean these do really further upend the balance between liberty on the one hand and security on the other.

LEWIS: Well, they do. They reduce judicial oversight with the secret intelligence courts instead of saying the court may do this now it’s the court will do this. They can have ex parte conversations where they go into the judge without anyone else around. In terms of information about detainees, not only can they detain anyone they’d like to detain, there is no public information about it.

Journalists cannot find out the names, so we detained over a thousand people after September 11th because we thought they might all be terrorists. Not one of them was really found with any criminal charges to be a terrorist. And we don’t know the names of almost all those people, still. And so it does appear that everything that folks might be concerned about with the Patriot Act, this is times five or times ten is what I look at it. I see it very serious.

MOYERS: You and I have had this kind of discussion often, we go back a long way together. The foundation that I serve on has been a big supporter of yours and you’ve been a big supporter of our journalism. If we were fighting terrorists instead of being journalists, wouldn’t we want this kind of power in our hands?

LEWIS: Well, we would, but we operate in a democracy and there’s other considerations. I mean I think, you know, there’s no question, if you’re in law enforcement, this is gonna make it easier for you to do your job. The problem is, we have a history in our country, just in our lifetime, in the last quarter century, where we’ve seen FBI and CIA abuses of ordinary citizens (THINK COVID/CBDC).

Where mail has been opened, where homes have been broken into. Where infiltration has occurred in political groups. Informants have been used, misused. People’s lives have been ruined. People have committed suicide because of the pressures brought against them by the government, by these kinds of secret intelligence agencies.

This is not a completely crazy idea to worry about the power of the government. And it was curbed and rolled back in the ’70s. And there is something obviously occurring here in the public space around the whole issue of liberty and security right now.

And it is clearly changing and it’s moving towards security. And the question for us as a people is what is the right balance. And I think my biggest personal concern is that there ought to be a debate about this. So the Patriot Act jammed through Congress in six weeks.

There was a Congressional — there was a Senate hearing that lasted an hour and a half, there were no questions to the Attorney General by the senators. This is too important for our country. Whatever anyone’s point of view, this should be a conversation that the country should have.

And I’m afraid they’re waiting for a war or something and then they’re gonna pop this baby out and then try to jam it through.

MOYERS: Foundation. And months from then, that foundation were deemed by the government or that organization were deemed by the government to have been in some way supporting terrorists, you could lose your citizenship because of your contribution, even if you didn’t know…

I mean, a lot of the question is if it does pass Congress, what would the courts do with it later.

I mean I think there are some legitimate issues there.

MOYERS: What do you make of this? This is the document that went from the Department of Justice with this draft legislation to certain very key people in government. Among them, Speaker of the House Dennis Hastert and Vice President Richard Cheney for their comments on this obviously confidential document. (LEADERS OF THE DEEP STATE)

LEWIS: It’s a way to say you’ve consulted Congress to some extent by sending it to the Speaker and not really consulting Congress.

As far as I can tell, and we have not polled every member or anything like that, but it appears that virtually no one on Capitol Hill, except for the Speaker, has seen this legislation. I’m talking about the people at the judiciary committees in the House and Senate don’t have this legislation. And have even been kind of yanked around a little bit for months about whether there will even be legislation.

MOYERS: Once upon a time, smallpox killed millions. Then we wiped it out — or so we thought.

That’s why the President recently ordered a massive smallpox vaccination program, and the debate has begun.

NOW producer William Brangham went to two states, Tennessee and New York, to see what’s going on.

BRANGHAM: Day one of the President’s vaccination drive last month was national news. The President is proposing that millions of people get inoculated for a disease that doesn’t exist in nature.

The disease is smallpox, one of the deadliest the world has ever seen, and one we thought we’d eliminated decades ago. But government officials worry the genie is back out of the bottle. They fear the smallpox virus is in the hands of people who will use it as a weapon.

PRESIDENT BUSH (FROM TAPE): One potential danger to America is the use of the smallpox virus as a weapon of terror.

BRANGHAM: So the President says, we simply have to prepare. But his plan has sparked a fierce debate: Just how real is the threat of a smallpox attack? What would happen if smallpox was used as a weapon? And are the steps we’re taking today the right way to prepare?

On one issue, there is universal agreement. The return of smallpox would be a nightmare. For those who don’t remember it smallpox was devastating. In the 20th century alone, it killed almost half a billion people.

SCHAFFNER: It’s one of the great pestilential diseases of humankind. The mortality rate was 30 to 40 percent. It’s an astounding illness.

BRANGHAM: Smallpox is once again worrying Dr. William Schaffner. He’s an infectious disease specialist at Vanderbilt University in Nashville, Tennessee.

SCHAFFNER: It was such an important disease that there were some societies where parents did not give their children formal names until the disease has come through, infected that child, and that child has survived. That was the extent of the impact of this disease in some societies.

BRANGHAM: Vivid depictions of smallpox — like these Aztec drawings — occur throughout history. Humanity was defenseless until 1796, when an English country doctor named Edward Jenner made a remarkable connection…

SCHAFFNER: He had heard rumors that milkmaids who got a disease called cowpox from milking cows were protected when smallpox went through the village. They didn’t get smallpox.

BRANGHAM: So Jenner wondered: Could he protect someone from smallpox by intentionally giving them cowpox? He tried out his theory on a young boy and when it worked, Jenner had found one of medicine’s greatest weapons. He’d invented vaccination.

SCHAFFNER: Jenner actually had the vision of eliminating this disease. He said, “I think I’ve now got the mechanism. All we need to do is apply it systematically.” It took the rest of us 150 years to pick up on the notion and do what Jenner had in mind.

BRANGHAM: Jenner’s dream began to come true in the 1960’s. The World Health Organization launched a massive campaign to take his vaccine to every corner of the world. If you’re over 30 years old, you’ve probably got that distinct, dime-sized scar on your shoulder or leg. After hundreds of millions of vaccinations the world finally conquered smallpox.

SCHAFFNER: There’s a paradox, isn’t there? Because everyone has said very consistently that the risk of a bio-terrorist event is low. There is no new information and there is no urgency. Yet, we have a program that would suggest that the risk is greater than that and there certainly is some urgency. Doesn’t jibe, does it?

BRANGHAM: Urgent or not, the old smallpox vaccine is coming out of deep freeze so America can begin a massive vaccination program.

The President’s plan is entirely voluntary. It’s broken down into three phases:

Phase 1 involves vaccinating half a million medical workers in public health offices and emergency rooms all over the country.

Phase 2 is a big step up. Ten million people would now get the vaccine. This would include even more medical workers, plus paramedics, police officers and firefighters.

And finally, phase 3 involves making the vaccine available to anyone in the country who wants it. Potentially hundreds of millions of doses.

But a lot of medical professionals are saying the President’s plan has significant problems. In fact, at least 80 hospitals around the country have said they’re not going to participate at all. Many that want to participate are finding it hard to recruit volunteers.

That’s because the smallpox vaccine is considered the most dangerous vaccine around — not because it gives you smallpox — remember, the vaccine is derived from the cowpox virus. But even that can cause serious problems. Complications can range from a mild rash and fever to severe swelling of the brain. BRANGHAM: New York City Health Commisioner Dr. Thomas Frieden thinks that’s a relatively controllable scenario.

FRIEDEN: Smallpox doesn’t spread readily between people. You have to be generally within six feet. The person only spreads it when he is or she is quite ill. They have a very apparent rash and very unusual rash on the face, high fever. So I’m not terribly concerned that smallpox would spread very widely in the city if it were introduced by individuals. If, on the other hand, terrorists have learned or designed a way to spray the smallpox virus, then it could spread more rapidly and it could be a much greater concern.

BRANGHAM: Turning smallpox into an airborne weapon is considered extremely difficult to do… and with no public knowledge of exactly who possesses the virus let alone who has the know-how to spread it, public health officials have adopted a range of responses. HESSLER: My perspective would probably be different if I was in a different part of the United States. In New York City, I was here. I saw two World Trade Center towers crumple to the ground that a month before that I would have told you they’ll be there forever. So today when I think of smallpox, I have to say it’s not impossible.

BRANGHAM: Bellevue is participating in phase one of the President’s plan — it will try to get a hundred or so staffers to volunteer to be vaccinated. Dr. Robert Hessler believes the risk of a smallpox attack is low, but says the hospital can’t afford to be caught off guard.

GOLDBERG: I think I have a responsibility. I’m not sure that by getting vaccinated, I’m serving that responsibility or providing the opposite, a disservice to the community by feeding into this — what I do see at times as a hysteria.

BRANGHAM: When you talk to people about the smallpox plan, the issue of money comes up a lot. For example: will hospitals or pharmaceutical companies be sued if someone gets sick from the vaccine? What about people who do get sick and have to be hospitalized or take time off of work? Will they be compensated?

The Homeland Security Act contains a liability provision – it shields the federal government and the vaccine manufacturers from any lawsuits arising out of this program. But individuals receive no such protection.

GOLDBERG: There seems to be a lot of support within our government to protect the drug companies, to protect the insurance companies, but not as much support to protect the individuals. And that’s definitely something that concerns me.

BRANGHAM: Money’s also a concern when you consider the total cost of the President’s plan. Its estimated that phases one and two could cost hundreds of millions of dollars. Perhaps a billion. The President expects the states to pay for it.

The Bush administration did give the states some money last year for biodefense but that money has basically been spent. There’s no new federal money coming for the smallpox plan. And that has some public health officials worried. They say, “look, twenty thousand people died last year from the flu. How are we supposed to tackle those known killers when we’re spending all this time and money on smallpox?”

SCHAFFNER: Just immunizing, vaccinating Phase One personnel has taken a huge commitment out of our public health staff around the country. Going beyond that to ten million is almost unthinkable. And, clearly, we’ve already diverted resources from other public health programs. And that will continue.

BRANGHAM: So that’s where it stands: the country is spending hundreds of millions of dollars to fight a disease that may never show up.

So most Americans are left weighing the unknowable risk of bioterrorism against the known risk of an old, but effective vaccine.

EDWARDS: This smallpox story has been an amazing example of how do you decide what risks and benefits are? How do you let people decide what risks and benefits that they want to assume? Because risk is personal. Risk is, you know, it’s not a one in a million. It’s yes or no.

Prigogine defined dissipative structures and their role in thermodynamic systems far from equilibrium, a discovery that won him the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1977. In summary, Ilya Prigogine discovered that the importation and dissipation of energy into chemical systems could result in the emergence of new structures (hence dissipative structures) due to internal self-reorganization. In his 1955 text, Prigogine drew connections between dissipative structures and the Rayleigh-Bénard instability, and the Turing mechanism.  Prigogine's theorem is germane to these ideas.

Mitochondria are dissipative structures in cells, but not the only ones.  They transform energy and create order from the disorder in light energy they use to operate.  The water mitochondria create is probably the single most important dissipative structure that life is based upon in cells.  According to Prigogine, determinism loses its explanatory power in the face of irreversibility and instability in dissipative systems. This is a major departure from the approach of Newton, Einstein, and Schrödinger, all of whom expressed their theories in terms of deterministic equations.

### Tweet 8

Indeterminism is the opposite of determinism and is related to chance.  Chance is related to probability. In science, most specifically quantum theory in physics links directly to probability and not cause and effect.  Indeterminism is the belief that no event is certain and the entire outcome of anything is probabilistic. Heisenberg's uncertainty principle and the "Born rule", proposed by Max Born, are often starting points in support of the indeterministic nature of the universe.  Indeterminism is also asserted by Sir Arthur Eddington and Murray Gell-Mann. Indeterminism has been promoted by the French biologist Jacques Monod's essay "Chance and Necessity". Ilya Prigogine argued for indeterminism in complex systems.

There is a well-known theorem of minimum entropy production derived by Prigogine, which states that entropy exported from a system reaches a minimum, or becomes zero, at thermodynamic equilibrium and at steady states close to thermodynamic equilibrium. Prigogine's theorem is a direct consequence of Onsager's reciprocity relationship which holds at steady states close to thermodynamic equilibrium. The principle of internal entropy compensation, is in addition to, and implies the principle of minimum entropy production, and may even be valid in regimes far from thermodynamic equilibrium.

# Thread: Measuring Radio Frequency (RF) EMF radiation can get complicated, and confusing.
**Source:** <https://threadreaderapp.com/thread/1694295687263830191.html>
**Published:** 2023-08-24T14:33:41.000Z
**Tweets:** 15
----------------------------------------------------------------------

### Tweet 1
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694295687263830191>

Measuring Radio Frequency (RF) EMF radiation can get complicated, and confusing. There are many different units of measurements to describe exposures, since harm varies with power, distance, source, modulation, and length of exposure. RF is also absorbed differently depending on the conductivity of different body parts. That being said, no set standard applies accurately across the board.
Because an RF electromagnetic field has both an electric and a magnetic component, the intensity of each field can be measured in different units, such as Electric Field Strength (volts per meter), Magnetic Field Strength (amps per meter) and Power Density (milliwatts per square centimeter), which takes into account both fields when measuring exposure farther away from the RF source.

### Tweet 2
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694296096158241050>

To measure the RF exposure coming from wireless handheld devices, the most universal standard is the Specific Absorption Rate, or SAR limit. SAR describes the speed at which the radio waves are absorbed in the body, in watts per kilogram. Any device that falls within the 900 MHz to 5,600 MHz (5.6GHz) wireless device frequency range is subject to the SAR standard. For more information about SAR read this.

### Tweet 3
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694296480918425674>

The International Commission on Non-Ionizing Radiation Protection (ICNIRP) is an NGO recognized by the World Health Organization (WHO). In 2011, radiation standards were created based on evaluations of the established health effects. These standards detail all types of radiation limits for different electromagnetic frequencies.

### Tweet 4
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694297045077500206>

For the FCC standard, no more than 1.6 watts per kilogram (energy) can be absorbed by any 1 gram of tissue. For the ICNIRP standard, no more than 2 watts per kilogram can be absorbed by 10 grams of contiguous tissue.
So which is better? Well, SAR values depend on average volume. Since the ICNIRP SAR standard covers a larger area of 10 grams of tissue, there is actually less allowable RF exposure per gram of tissue.
In the end, SAR was developed only to minimize thermal effects of RF emissions on the body, and doesn’t take into account biological effects that might happen regardless of tissue heating.

### Tweet 5
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694297577724781007>

In terms of other EMF exposure regulations, much of the world has adopted some version of the complete ICNIRP guidelines, but some countries are stricter than others in implementation and establishing specific standards.
The WHO chart below details limits for Power Density, Electric Field, and SAR in many different countries.

### Tweet 6
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694298385400971418>

Very few countries have adopted the precaution principle in protecting health first. Vulnerable populations like kids and the sick should have strict guidelines.

### Tweet 7
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694299845400388002>

The FCC hasn’t updated radiation standards since 1996 in the USA a time where only 15% of Americans owned a cell phone compared to 96% (~315 million) of Americans today. Like mentioned earlier, cell phones are required to have a SAR value of 1.6 watts or less per kilogram of body weight. Today just based on the inverse square law and population density the US government is now ignoring the basic physics of safety for economic progress. This is short sighted in light of the diseases it causes.

### Tweet 8
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694300703232016546>

Belgium has some of the strictest radiation regulations. Belgium banned marketing of cell phones to children below age 14, and banned the sale of phones designed for children below age 7.
Its capital, Brussels, mandates only 6 volts per meter (Electric Field Limit) for public RF exposure, which is 50 times stricter than international standards.  Serious countries need to go lower for higher risk populations

### Tweet 9
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694301150491680868>

Italy is another country with stricter regulations than ICNIRP mandates. There are two different limits set by Italian law.
General limits are about 30% lower and restrictive limits, which apply to “buildings for human long-stay purposes” are 10 times lower than ICNIRP standards.
Beyond federally enforced restrictions, there are local regulations establishing distances between base sites and sensitive locations–think schools, hospitals, etc. For example, in Rome, there must be at least 100 meters between a base site and a sensitive place.

### Tweet 10
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694301763669537152>

Switzerland, Belgium, Italy, Russia, and countries in Eastern Europe are questioning if RF radiation emissions are safe, and some are using precautionary measures to protect their populations and environments as EMF radiation and 5G is researched more. However, many in these countries still battle to be at the front of technological innovation for the sake of political and economic power, and this might get in the way of safe RF standards. Putin just made the decision to remove all 5G technologies this month in Russia.

### Tweet 11
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694302885138289034>

The 2017 NTP study in the USA points out that the FCC is asleep at the wheel on health implications This study results is beyond concerning.

### Tweet 12
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694303873660313665>

This rate is measured as the power absorbed within a defined area of body tissue in a standard measurement of watts per kilogram (W/kg).
SAR can be an average measure over the entire body, or a small sample amount. What you get is the greatest level measured in a defined area of the body.
SAR  measures exposure within the frequency range of 100 kHz and 10 GHz which includes electronic devices such as laptops, tablets, WiFi routers and cell phones.
The value is highly dependent upon the body parts being tested, energy levels and the proximity to the radiating source.
The SAR value is established by selecting areas of the body to be tested, finding the highest absorption rate while exposing that area closest to the radiating source.
There are guidelines for SAR established by national and international organizations.
For example, below are SAR requirements for cell phones, set in 1993, which became adopted by the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) in 1996. Back then, only a small fraction of the population were using cell phones, and only for minutes of the day.

### Tweet 13
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694306087095222532>

In the US, the Federal Communication Commission (FCC) requires that cell phones have a SAR level at or below 1.6 watts per kilogram (W/kg) taken over a volume containing a mass of 1 gram (g) of tissue.
European Union has lower SAR limits set than the U.S.
The EU has set SAR limits for mobile phones (and other such hand-held devices), to be less than or equal to 2 W/kg averaged over 10 g of tissue.

### Tweet 15
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694309940309942336>

[ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/P…](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4440565/)

### Tweet 16
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1694310375292874925>

# Thread: The higher the refractive index of a medium the slower light travels in it.
**Source:** <https://threadreaderapp.com/thread/1686811435593064448.html>
**Published:** 2023-07-29T00:40:02.000Z
**Tweets:** 8
----------------------------------------------------------------------

### Tweet 1
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1686811435593064448>

The higher the refractive index of a medium the slower light travels in it. This carries implications for physiology. Bulk water with 155 ppm of deuterium has a refractive index of 1.33. EZ water is at 1.46 which means metabolic water slows light down before it is used in… https://t.co/Zcz72tZ9vt[twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1686811435593064448)

**Source:** <https://threadreaderapp.com/thread/1672941128268611584.html>
**Published:** 2023-06-26T12:41:02.000Z

### Tweet 1
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1672941128268611584>

Why doesn't centralized science understand the retina, eye clock, and FM radio station function of the central retinohypothalamic tract well today?

### Tweet 2
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1672941616833724416>

To survive in adverse environments organisms must be able to perceive and respond to their surroundings. One of the most complex and remarkable sensory systems utilized in the animal kingdom is vision. Through specialized cells (photoreceptors) animals can make use of light… https://t.co/poNQcrKGm2[twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1672941616833724416)

### Tweet 3
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1672942021877612546>

The eye allows for the detection and processing of detailed light signals. Representatives of Cnidaria, Mollusca, Annelida, Onychophora, Arthropoda, and Chordata are examples of organisms that have developed complex eyes, often associated with image formation (Zuker, 1994).… https://t.co/s8bdLq5e2S[twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1672942021877612546)

### Tweet 4
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1672942294499041281>

With rare exceptions, vertebrates possess a complex camera-style eye that is remarkably conserved in form and function throughout the group. The retina of the vertebrate eye is specialized to form detailed images, detect motion, enhance contrast, and/or perceive color… https://t.co/oWKS3G0uqQ[twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1672942294499041281)

### Tweet 5
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1672943120927866882>

Contributing to the struggle to understand vertebrate eye evolution is the complexity of the organ itself. To receive light signals the retina contains highly specialized photoreceptor cells for light detection (these cells require a mechanism for light transduction and… https://t.co/8JQd7UcehO[twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1672943120927866882)

### Tweet 6
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1672945320789934080>

In addition, the structure of the camera-style eye is also optimized to direct light onto the retina (to increase visual acuity using OPN 1 &2). Structures such as the pupil and lens focus incoming light onto the retinal photoreceptors, pigment provides directionality for… https://t.co/ay9HIQWJS0[twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1672945320789934080)

### Tweet 7
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1672946680138805249>

Another factor confounding a complete understanding of eye evolution is how abruptly the eye appears within the vertebrate phylogeny and the lack of transitional states. This was expertly covered by Lamb et al., in 2007. From that paper comes the two slides below. This is… https://t.co/zFoWIVoHpm[twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1672946680138805249)

### Tweet 8
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1672948500844167168>

The key to understanding the human eye was found in understanding the physics that built it and finding the animal that evolved before the light changed. That task fell right in my lap in the halls of that museum when I learned about the non-jawed fish called the lamprey and… https://t.co/ij8QNyKeQ7[twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1672948500844167168)

### Tweet 9
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1672949604764057600>

How did vertebrate eyes begin?  The lesson comes from the hagfish and lampreys.

The vertebrate eye is a vital sensory organ that has long fascinated scientists, but the details of how this organ evolved are still unclear. The vertebrate eye is distinct from the simple… https://t.co/lNmhFvXAxf[twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1672949604764057600)

### Tweet 10
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1672950751826411521>

Hagfish eyes are markedly basic compared to the eyes of other vertebrates, lacking a pigmented epithelium, a lens, and a retinal architecture built of three cell layers: the photoreceptors, interneurons, and ganglion cells. Concomitant with hagfish belonging to the… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1672950751826411521)

### Tweet 11
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1672952105152176129>

Modern humans are new phylogenetic creatures in evolution and they have created a new version of light which is sculpting their brains and colony of mitochondria to create new things in biology. Today all those new things manifest as neolithic diseases that centralized… https://t.co/0fGyCNLyLa[twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1672952105152176129)

### Tweet 12

# Thread: During the 20th century, the study of UV radiation led to the development of different terminologies between sciences.
**Source:** <https://threadreaderapp.com/thread/1665893845710655491.html>
**Published:** 2023-06-01T13:40:48.000Z
**Tweets:** 16
----------------------------------------------------------------------

### Tweet 1
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665893845710655491>

During the 20th century, the study of UV radiation led to the development of different terminologies between sciences. Physicists developed a terminology based on the physical properties of UV radiation. They adopted the term ‘‘near UV’’ to refer to solar UV that reaches the… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665893845710655491)

### Tweet 2
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665895833882034177>

Centralized science says."given the sun’s importance and our… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665895833882034177)

### Tweet 3
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665896780150472704>

Around 400 BCE, two events of scientific importance occurred in Greece. The Ionian philosopher Anaxagoras was put on trial for promoting the idea that the sun is a big fiery rock, rather than a deity, and the Athenian physician Hippocrates prescribed heliotherapy (sunbathing)… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665896780150472704)

### Tweet 4
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665898297318424579>

Although heliotherapy has been practiced by humans for at least 2400 years, there was very little objective evidence supporting its purported use. As soon as man invented the power grid to power artificial light, JP Morgan turned it into a profitable business on Wall St.… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665898297318424579)

### Tweet 5
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665900024255979521>

In the 18th century, many reports began to appear in the medical literature indicating that sunlight ameliorated different skin diseases. In 1735, Fiennius described a case in which he cured a cancerous growth on a patient’s lip using a sunbath. In 1774, Faure reported that he… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665900024255979521)

### Tweet 6
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665901774035615744>

In 1843, Scharling measured reduced production of CO2 in subjects at night, and in 1866 von Pettenkofer and Voit reported that serum bicarbonate levels were lower at night. In 1850, Berthold found that hair production was greater in the daytime, and in 1888 Fere ́ noted that… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665901774035615744)

### Tweet 7
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665905757873733632>

In the 19th century most UV light was manufactured using arc lights. Full spectrum light was not used and issues mounted with the use of these manufactured lights were and generalized & conflated to be equivalent to the sun's ability to create UV light from 1911 onward.… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665905757873733632)

### Tweet 8
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665907367316189192>

In 1824, Edwards reported that sunlight enhanced the rate of development of frog eggs. Twenty- six years later, Higginbothom showed that the development of frog and salamander eggs progressed normally in the dark as long as the temperature was controlled. In 1858, Beclard… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665907367316189192)

### Tweet 9
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665908890318282752>

In 1858, Beclard noted that violet-blue light enhanced CO2 production in adult frogs but not in the birds or mammals he tested. In 1870, Selmi and Piacentini reported that yellow light, the complementary color to blue light enhanced CO2 production in a dog, hen, and turtle. In… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665908890318282752)

### Tweet 10
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665910664873881600>

In 1883, Lubbock showed that ants are able to see UV rays, and in 1914 Van Herwerden found that Daphnia (water fleas) responded to rays shorter than 334 nm. In 1924, von Frisch demonstrated that bees can perceive rays at 300 nm, and Lutz confirmed that bees, wasps, and fruit… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665910664873881600)

### Tweet 11
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665913023439970305>

Several investigators studied the influence of light on blinded animals. In 1876, Fubini showed that blinded frogs put on more weight than normal frogs when both were raised under identical lighting conditions. This was a seminal paper in the Leptin Rx creation. Both groups… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665913023439970305)

### Tweet 12
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665914227784073217>

Downes and Blunt made one of the most influential discoveries in all of photobiology. They noticed that direct sunlight inhibited the growth of microorganisms in test tubes containing Pasteur solution. Illumination for several hours resulted in test tubes free of bacteria for… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665914227784073217)

### Tweet 13
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665915224996691970>

Between 1893 and 1895, Ward performed a remarkable series of experiments demonstrating superb technical skill and ingenuity. Using improved versions of Buchner’s assay and Geisler’s apparatus, he showed that violet-blue and near UV (UVA 380nm) rays were the most damaging part… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665915224996691970)

### Tweet 14
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665918046123245568>

Webb reviewed the literature showing that UVA rays cause lethal and mutagenic effects in microorganisms even in the absence of exogenous photosensitizers. Unlike UVB effects, UVA effects are oxygen-dependent. In 1980, D’Aoust and colleagues showed that flavins are endogenous… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665918046123245568)

### Tweet 15
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665921380527886336>

I hope you can see looking back at medical history is a huge benefit to see what we've missed and discarded because we did not understand the physics of organisms. The discovery of UV radiation and its effects on living organisms was a gradual process that involved… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1665921380527886336)

### Tweet 16
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1665921785383067649>

Lesson over.

**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1647988945777508352>

Cultured human skin cells keratinocytes & melanocytes, can produce and express POMC; POMC expression is enhanced by exposure to UVB, through a cyclic AMP-dependent pathway; & the action of UVB on POMC production involves a cellular response to oxidative stress.

### Tweet 2
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1647989336732729344>

It's been demonstrated in the literature that UVB radiation stimulates increased expression of the proopiomelanocortin gene. This is accompanied by production and release of alpha-melanocyte stimulating hormone (alpha-MSH) and adrenocorticotropin (ACTH) by both normal and… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1647989336732729344)

### Tweet 3

### Tweet 4
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1647991216074629121>

N-acetyl-cysteine, a precursor of glutathione (GSH), is an intracellular free radical scavenger & abolishes the UVB-stimulated POMC peptide production and secretion. This is why taking NAC in sunlight is a real bad idea. MDs in the Phillipines inject glutathione to remove… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1647991216074629121)

### Tweet 5

POMC and MCR1 genes are regulated in response to UV irradiation by p38 stress-activated kinase signaling to the transcription factor, upstream stimulating factor-1. Did you know Upstream transcription factor 1 also is the first gene associated with familial combined… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1647993477697863681)

### Tweet 6
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1647994991233343488>

Thus, USF1 and USF2 transcription factors are critical for the adenomatous polyposis coli (APC) gene and its expression. So this implies that a lack of sunlight is also linked to colon cancer risk. Do you see how these pieces fit in diseases? What are the implications?… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1647994991233343488)

### Tweet 7
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1647997336792997888>

The literature also shows that overexpression of USF1 or USF2 enhances the basal expression of HO-1. The human HO-1 (heme oxygenase-1) gene encodes a microsomal enzyme responsible for the breakdown of heme. A lack of sunlight epigenetically affects the expression USF1/2 to… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1647997336792997888)

### Tweet 8

Adding NaCl back to the blood can improve this situation. Did you know that? Did you know sunlight helps lower lipid deposition in arteries, retina, heart, kidney and liver. Amazing how the sun and melanin operate in unison to control many of the things our tissues need to… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1647998728563720193)

### Tweet 9

# Thread: Sun is fired up for my current Patreon series on how she runs mammals.
**Source:** <https://threadreaderapp.com/thread/1645807300526096385.html>
**Published:** 2023-04-12T10:33:23.000Z
**Tweets:** 8
----------------------------------------------------------------------

### Tweet 1
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645807300526096385>

Sun is fired up for my current Patreon series on how she runs mammals.

### Tweet 2
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645808963823501312>

Feeding behavior is under the control of central retinal pathway hypothalamic circuits. In this circuit, arcuate nucleus, activated agouti-related peptide expressing neurons promote food intake while POMC-producing neurons promote satiety.

### Tweet 3
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645813815915732992>

Homeostatic feeding regulation can be disrupted by exogenous substances, such as cannabinoids. Activation of cannabinoid receptor 1 (CB1R) can lead to robust feeding despite animals being sated. This has big implications.

### Tweet 4
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645814705448005633>

Hypothalamic POMC neurons promote satiety. Cannabinoid receptor 1 is critical for the central regulation of food intake. Data now reveals that CB1R-controlled feeding is paralleled by decreased activity of POMC neurons. So those who stay up all night on the internet or…

### Tweet 5
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645815315844964352>

Chemical promotion of CB1R activity increased feeding, and strikingly, CB1R activation also promoted the neuronal activity of POMC cells. This paradoxical increase in POMC activity was crucial for CB1R-induced feeding, because…

### Tweet 6
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645815770830499842>

Because POMC gene encodes both the anorexigenic peptide, α-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (α-MSH), and the peptide, β-endorphin. CB1R activation selectively increased β-endorphin peptide but not α-MSH release in the hypothalamus

### Tweet 7
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645816321127374848>

The systemic or hypothalamic administration of the opioid receptor antagonist, naloxone, blocked acute CB1R-induced feeding.

### Tweet 8
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645818132693491714>

These processes involved mitochondrial redox adaptations, which, when blocked, abolished CB1R-induced cellular responses and feeding. Taken together the data unmasked a previously unsuspected role of POMC neurons in the promotion of feeding by cannabinoids. This tells you…

----------------------------------------------------------------------

### Tweet 1
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645457083960049665>

It is very rare these days to see children with untreated PKU but when I was a resident at Charity Hospital I got a chance to see several of them and it was always remarkable how those cases mimicked Parkinson's Disease and showed up rapidly in a child.

### Tweet 2
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645457485912760321>

Kids with untreated PKU appeared normal in the first few months of life until it's noticed that they fail to achieve developmental milestones in time and their head circumference does not grow and their skin is pale and afflicted by breakouts.

### Tweet 3
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645458088927846401>

The brain remains atrophic and mimics a lack of development that we see in neurodegeneration in older humans. Most untreated children come to medical attention after 6–9 months of life because of developmental delay, they have a musty body odor, microcephaly due to rapid…

### Tweet 4
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645461810286415876>

Their complexion is striking because it is usually much lighter than their parents and siblings secondary to the associated inhibition of tyrosinase and low tyrosine levels. This key finding on the skin is a huge tell that the skin is atrophic like the brain because of a lack…

### Tweet 5
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645462284339118084>

These children often suffer from massive eczema. Skin issues are a tell in kids. In older untreated children, severe intellectual disability, epilepsy, aggressive behavior, self-mutilation, autistic features, schizophrenia-like symptoms, and Parkinson-like features are…

### Tweet 6
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1645463262962630657>

These children are striking because many facets of this disease resemble modern neolithic diseases.  PKU is an inborn error of metabolism but I believe the blue light hazard causes the same phenotype in adults although the presentation is never as abrupt or as devastating as…

# Thread: Today's lesson on Quantum Biology: Most centralized neuroscientists with government funding believe that the brain opera
**Source:** <https://threadreaderapp.com/thread/1638656642488360960.html>
**Published:** 2023-03-24T14:29:31.000Z
**Tweets:** 30
----------------------------------------------------------------------

### Tweet 1
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638656642488360960>

Today's lesson on Quantum Biology: Most centralized neuroscientists with government funding believe that the brain operates in a classical manner. Are they right? Is there experimental proof they are wrong already?

### Tweet 2
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638657431801585664>

Supercomputers can beat us at chess & perform more calculations per second than our brains. But there are other tasks our brains perform that dust silicon-based computers; ie: interpreting events and situations, using imagination, creativity, and problem-solving skills.

### Tweet 3
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638658092954181636>

The mammalian brain is a powerful computer, which uses not just neurons but the connections between the neurons to process and interpret information.
And then there is consciousness, neuroscience’s giant question mark. What causes it? Is it classical or quantum?

### Tweet 4
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638658369065033728>

Recently, scientists at Trinity College Dublin, using a technique to test for quantum gravity, suggested that entanglement may be at work within our brains. Oops said the Ivory Tower biology krewe.

### Tweet 5
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638658914521690113>

Some of these mechanisms might help the brain process the world around it through sensory input. There are also certain isotopes in our brain whose spins change how our body and brain react. For example, Lithium does this in bipolar disorder.

### Tweet 6
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638659737284759553>

This isotope thing Uncle Jack, explain? Xenon has a nuclear spin of 1/2 & has anesthetic properties, while xenon with no spin cannot. And various isotopes of lithium with different spins change development and parenting ability in mammals, FYI

### Tweet 7
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638660333567987712>

In humans, this mechanism can affect reality perception using the quantum abilities of nuclear spin. Since nuclear spin is an inherent quantum property that affects how long each atom can remain coherent

### Tweet 8
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638660560114921478>

That is, isolated from its environment, it could be linked to how signaling in neurons is controlled because the nervous system is the source of both electric and magnetic fields from our mitochondria and chromophore proteins as an excitable tissue with high mitochondrial… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1638660560114921478)

### Tweet 9
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638660704684089344>

The spin state of hydrogen plays a major role in these biophysical effects. These are effects BIOCHEMISTS WHIFF on because of their own dogma. [ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/3019440](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/3019440)

### Tweet 10
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638661183191523328>

Despite such intriguing findings, the brain is largely assumed to be a classical system by the modern neurobiology community and centralized healthcare paradigm.

### Tweet 11
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638661542890577926>

Question: If our brain uses quantum computation, might those quantum operators be different from operators known from atomic systems?

### Tweet 12
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638661791042379776>

Quantum gravity is another example in quantum physics where we do not yet know what we are dealing with.

There are two main realms of physics. There is the physics of the tiny microscopic world — the atoms and photons, particles and waves that interact and behave very unlike… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1638661791042379776)

### Tweet 13
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638661970483093504>

Then there is the realm of gravity, which governs the motion of planets and stars and keeps us humans stuck to Earth. Unifying these realms under an overarching theory is where quantum gravity comes in — it will help scientists understand the underlying forces that govern our… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1638661970483093504)

### Tweet 14
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638662171205971970>

Since quantum gravity and quantum processes in the brain are both big unknowns, the researchers decided to use the same method other scientists are using to try to understand quantum gravity.

### Tweet 15
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638662995638099969>

What was their plan? They used an MRI just like I do in my clinic. Did you know that using an MRI can show entanglement? With an MRI that can sense entanglement, they looked to see whether proton spins in our brain could interact & become entangled through an unknown… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1638662995638099969)

### Tweet 16
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638663391546859522>

Similar to the research for quantum gravity, the goal was to understand an unknown system. The unknown system may interact with known systems like the proton spins within the brain. If the unknown system can mediate entanglement to the known system, then, it has been shown,… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1638663391546859522)

### Tweet 17
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638663978694893570>

What did they do? The researchers scanned 40 subjects with an MRI. Then they watched what happened, and correlated the activity with the patient’s heartbeat.

### Tweet 18
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638664224175194118>

The heartbeat is not just the motion of an organ within our body. Rather, the heart, like many other parts of our body, is engaged in two-way communication with the brain via the vagus nerve. Both organs both send each other signals.

### Tweet 19
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638664466366603265>

As the heart beats, it generates a signal called the heartbeat potential, or HEP. With each peak of the HEP, the researchers saw a corresponding spike in the NMR signal, which corresponds to the interactions among proton spins.

### Tweet 20
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638664824258285569>

Might isotopic effects cause some quantum change in a material? Might the size variation of isotopes found in the blood be linked by Nature to make circulatory blood act differently than water in other parts of our body? How about H2Oin our mitochondrial matrix? Do you know… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1638664824258285569)

### Tweet 21
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638665076864430082>

The lower the nuclear spin of an atom or a particle, the less the nucleus of this object interacts with electric & magnetic fields. The less a particle or atom reacts to a field the more time it can spend being coherent in a system.

### Tweet 22
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638665410148016128>

Does our mitochondria interact this way with our food too Jack? Yep. Is this why saturated fats favor protium over deuterium in Nature?
Deuterium is an isotope with spin = 1, unlike hydrogen-1, which has spin = 1/2 = Why guys like Dr. Attia have a lot to learn still about… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1638665410148016128)

### Tweet 23
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638665798565650435>

The physicists knew this MRI signal could be a result of entanglement, and witnessing it might indicate there was indeed a non-classical intermediary between the two organs.

### Tweet 24
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638666333452730370>

Explain Jack. OK. The HEP is an electrophysiological event, like alpha or beta waves in EEG. So a HEP is tied to consciousness because it depends on awareness. Similarly, the MRI signal indicating entanglement was only present during conscious awareness, which was… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1638666333452730370)

### Tweet 25
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638666886551330816>

Seeing entanglement in the brain may show that the brain is not classical, as previously thought, but rather a powerful quantum system. Since the results were confirmed, we now have data/proof that the brain uses quantum processes. Jack says to the centralized system......

### Tweet 26
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638667309748310016>

When you know better your patients can do better. I go where no classical researchers or clinicians will go because they fear what they do not know. Me.....I'm a curious MFer so I am going where science takes me.

### Tweet 27
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638667800053989377>

The longer a particle or atom or compound can remain coherent the longer time it can spend being entangled. The more neurons are entangled or linked the better they seem to function. This paper from 1986 shows us all experimental proof of this effect. [ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/3019440](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/3019440)

### Tweet 28
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638668351126794241>

So if you've been following me closely I bet you see why now mitochondria picked H+ as a fuel source and why glycolysis has so many enzymes in it to process foods. Our system wants H+ to become quantum coherent and be able to entangle. See that, you're not a dumb ass.

### Tweet 29
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638668754874691584>

I had to inject a little saucy humor. If you liked this lesson come join me at my classroom for more cool lessons on how you really work at [patreon.com/DrJackKruse](http://www.patreon.com/DrJackKruse) Thanks for reading.

**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638526948270919680>

Passion and satisfaction go hand in hand, and without them, any happiness is only temporary, because there's nothing to make it last. You must care deeply about yourself first to finally tap into a passion.

**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638527242362912768>

**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638527584341295110>

Those transients help raise our dopamine levels to push us to action. Your goal in connecting with nature is to share that heat and stop allowing the passers-by only to see a wisp of smoke in your soul. ### Tweet 4
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638527828814602240>

Hope is just a dulled passion for the possible. Tap that hope by doing until you find it. Once the rain starts falling in our life it's hard to tell it to stop because of our expectations. Caution is always the path to mediocrity and I have a sense that might be why most have… [twitter.com/i/web/status/1…](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1638527828814602240)

### Tweet 5
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1638528303031091201>

Take risks. Stop riding the middle ground and reach beyond, by doing things you don't think you can. Gliding on the winds of life creates a passionless mediocrity and that is all most people think they can achieve from the middle ground.

### Tweet 1
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1637975702308831238>

Today's lesson: Perspective & vision are critical. Science provides us with no facts; it provides us only with a perspective that shapes interpretations that show up in journals and textbooks.

### Tweet 2
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1637975874694774784>

The greatest risk to mankind in science today is not that we aim too high and miss the truth, but that we aim too low and hits targets that keep us from nature's truth.

### Tweet 3
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1637976078370078723>

People do not realize how important our perceptions and beliefs are in science. Roses are gorgeous when we observe them on a bush, right?

### Tweet 4
**Source:** <https://twitter.com/DrJackKruse/status/1637976262248374273>

So beautiful that we cut them from their life force to give them to people we care for. This raises an interesting question: If all we had were roses in the world, would the thorns then be beautiful too????
